title: Non-Abelian braiding in Abelian Fractional Quantum Hall Phases from realistic interactions
abstract: We propose a method of realizing non-Abelian braiding of fractionalized quasiholes in the Laughlin fractional quantum Hall phase at $ν=1/3$ with realistic two-body interactions within the lowest Landau level. It is numerically shown that low-lying gapped excitations near $ν=1/3$ are contained almost entirely within the null space of the three-body Moore-Read model Hamiltonian. They are thus quantum fluids of non-Abelian quasiholes that are in principle physically accessible. In particular, Laughlin ground state can be described as a fluid of ``$ψ$-type" quasiholes formed by binding a magnetic flux with a Majorana fermion (MF), and the Laughlin quasiholes are described by the ``$1$-type'' quasiholes, which are magnetic fluxes without a MF attached. Within the Laughlin phase, Laughlin quasiholes can be locally fractionalized into non-Abelian quasiholes, when the strong attraction between them is overcome by properly designed one-body electronstatic trapping potentials. Extensive numerics with proper finite-size scaling corroborate this physical picture, and our study points to the possibility of realizing non-Abelian braiding within an Abelian topological phase in experiment without the need for fine-tuning realistic electron-electron interaction.
summary:
The research demonstrates that non-Abelian braiding statistics can be engineered in the seemingly simple Laughlin FQH state at $
u=1/3$. By applying localized trapping potentials, the system forces the fractionalized quasiholes to behave as non-Abelian anyons, providing a pathway to realizing topological quantum computation using realistic electron interactions.
main_problem: The paper proposes a method to realize non-Abelian braiding of fractionalized quasiholes within the Abelian Laughlin Fractional Quantum Hall (FQH) phase.
main_result:
Non-Abelian statistics can be realized in $
u=1/3$ by inducing fractionalization of Laughlin quasiholes using carefully designed one-body trapping potentials, without requiring fine-tuning of realistic electron-electron interactions.
method: The study employs numerical simulations (Exact Diagonalization and variational energy calculations) on spherical geometry to analyze the low-lying excitations relative to both two-body pseudopotentials ($\hat{V}_{2bdy}$) and three-body terms ($\hat{V}_{3bdy}$).
model_or_system:
The system is the Fractional Quantum Hall state at $
u=1/3$, modeled using concepts derived from the Laughlin and Moore-Read Hamiltonians. The analysis focuses on quasihole excitations, which are shown to fractionalize into non-Abelian anyons ($\sigma$-type) when subjected to external trapping potentials.
key_observables: Low-lying excitation energies, ground state degeneracy (comparing Abelian vs. non-Abelian expectations), and the ratio of energy gaps used to diagnose topological order.
important_parameters:
Filling factor $
u=1/3$, pinning strength ($\lambda$), and the nature of the trapping potential (Gaussian vs. orbital).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on mapping complex interactions onto effective Hamiltonians in the Lowest Landau Level (LLL) basis, sometimes using idealized 'toy model' potentials to simulate realistic effects.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the energy spectra evolution as pinning strength increases, showing phase transitions marked by gap closing and changes in ground state degeneracy, confirming fractionalization.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the theoretical framework (Laughlin vs. MR models) to applying numerical techniques using external potentials ($\hat{V}_{1bdy}$) to induce quasihole fractionalization, culminating in evidence for non-Abelian braiding signatures.
why_interesting:
This work directly addresses how topological order, typically associated with exotic Hamiltonians like the Moore-Read model, can emerge robustly within a simpler, Abelian phase ($
u=1/3$) by engineering local potentials. This has direct implications for designing experimental quantum simulators.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. The Laughlin state as a fluid of Moore-Read quasi- holes: as the filling factor decreases from ν = 1/2, quasiholes of the MR states are created, which can be of one of the three types labelled 1, ψ, or σ. At filling factor ν = 1/3, two-body interaction favors ψ, which form a fluid that makes up the Laughlin ground state. Additional quasiholes in the Laughlin state must be 1-quasiholes (see discussion in main text.)

FIG. 2. The spectrum of ˆV 2bdy 1 on the sphere with 10 elec- trons and 28, 29, and 30 orbitals (Laughlin ground state, one- quasihole state, and two-quasihole state), respectively. The lowest 1000 eigenvalues are shown in each plot. The color bar shows variational energy of each eigenstate with respect to the Moore-Read model Hamiltonian, ˆV 3bdy 3 . All numerical results in this paper are given in the unit ℏ= c = e = B = 1.

FIG. 3. Finite-size scaling of difference in self-energy between 1-quasihole and ψ-quasihole (blue) and interaction energy of ψ-quasiholes at filling factor ν = 1/3, both calculated for a background ˆV 2bdy 1 interaction [48]. Dotted line shows linear fit.

FIG. 4. (a) A schematic diagram showing fractionalization of an Abelian quasihole by electrostatic potential induced by two wide STM tips (b) Spectrum of Eq. (5) at increasing λ when two potential pins are applied to a system with one quasihole. The color of each point reflects the nature of the correspond- ing eigenstate: a Laughlin quasihole positioned at a poten- tial pin (orange), a Laughlin quasihole positioned elsewhere (green), gapped neutral excitations (purple), or a strong mix- ing of the Laughlin quasihole with gapped excitations (blue). The critical point at which a phase transition to fractionalized Laughlin quasihole occurs is marked in red (see discussion in main text). (c)...
